content.title: Linear Functional
content.content:
A **linear functional** on a vector 
space $V$ is a linear map $f: V \to \FF$.

content.title: Dual Space
content.content:
The **dual space** of $V$, denoted $V^*$, is 
the set of all linear functionals on $V$:
\[
V^* = \mathcal{L}(V, \FF)
\]

content.title: Dual Space Dimension
content.content: If $V$ is finite-dimensional, then $\dim V^* = \dim V$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Dual Basis
content.content:
Let $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ be a basis for $V$. The **dual basis** 
$(e_1^*, \ldots, e_n^*)$ for $V^*$ is defined by
\[
e_i^*(e_j) = \delta_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & i = j \\ 0 & i \neq j \end{cases}
\]

content.title: Dual Basis Properties
content.content:
The dual basis $(e_1^*, \ldots, e_n^*)$ is indeed a basis for $V^*$. 

For any $v = \sum_{j=1}^n v_j e_j$, we have $e_i^*(v) = v_i$ 
(the $i$-th coordinate).

For any $f \in V^*$, we have $f = \sum_{i=1}^n f(e_i) e_i^*$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Dual of $\RR^n$
content.problem:
For the standard basis $(e_1, \ldots, e_n)$ of $\RR^n$, the dual 
functional $e_i^*$ extracts the $i$-th component: $e_i^*(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = x_i$.

content.title: Canonical Embedding
content.content:
There is a natural (basis-independent) linear map $\iota: V \to V^{**}$ 
defined by
\[
\iota(v)(f) = f(v) \quad \text{for } v \in V, f \in V^*
\]
This map is injective. If $V$ is finite-dimensional, it is an isomorphism.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
*Well-defined and linear:* For fixed $v$, the map 
$f \mapsto f(v)$ is linear in $f$, so $\iota(v) \in V^{**}$. 
Linearity of $\iota$ follows from linearity of evaluation.

*Injective:* If $\iota(v) = 0$, then $f(v) = 0$ for all $f \in V^*$. 
If $v \neq 0$, extend $\{v\}$ to a basis; the dual functional for $v$ 
satisfies $f(v) = 1 \neq 0$. Contradiction.

*Isomorphism:* $\dim V = \dim V^* = \dim V^{**}$ for 
finite-dimensional $V$, and an injective linear map between spaces 
of equal dimension is surjective.

The isomorphism $V \cong V^*$ requires choosing a basis; the 
isomorphism $V \cong V^{**}$ does not. This "naturality" makes 
$V^{**}$ the preferred way to identify $V$ with its bidual.

content.title: Transpose
content.content:
Let $T: V \to W$ be linear. The **transpose** 
(or **dual map**) $T^t: W^* \to V^*$ is defined by
\[
T^t(g) = g \circ T \quad \text{for } g \in W^*
\]
That is, $(T^t g)(v) = g(Tv)$.

content.title: Properties of Transpose
content.content:
1. $(S \circ T)^t = T^t \circ S^t$

2. $(T^t)^t = T$ (under the canonical identification $V \cong V^{**}$)

3. $\rank(T^t) = \rank(T)$

4. $\ker(T^t) = (\im T)^\circ$ (the annihilator)

5. $\im(T^t) = (\ker T)^\circ$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Annihilator
content.content:
For a subspace $W \subseteq V$, the **annihilator** is
\[
W^\circ = \{f \in V^* : f(w) = 0 \text{ for all } w \in W\}
\]

content.title: Tensor Product (Informal)
content.content:
The **tensor product** $V \otimes W$ is a 
vector space equipped with a bilinear map $\otimes: V \times W \to V \otimes W$ 
such that every bilinear map from $V \times W$ factors uniquely through it.

content.title: Universal Property
content.content:
For any bilinear map $B: V \times W \to U$, there exists a unique 
linear map $\tilde{B}: V \otimes W \to U$ such that 
$B(v, w) = \tilde{B}(v \otimes w)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Tensor Product Dimension
content.content:
$\dim(V \otimes W) = \dim V \cdot \dim W$.

If $(e_1, \ldots, e_m)$ is a basis for $V$ and $(f_1, \ldots, f_n)$ 
is a basis for $W$, then $\{e_i \otimes f_j\}$ is a basis for $V \otimes W$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Tensor Product Properties
content.content:
1. $V \otimes W \cong W \otimes V$ (via $v \otimes w \mapsto w \otimes v$)

2. $(U \otimes V) \otimes W \cong U \otimes (V \otimes W)$

3. $V \otimes \FF \cong V$

4. $(V \oplus W) \otimes U \cong (V \otimes U) \oplus (W \otimes U)$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Tensors and Matrices
content.problem:
$\RR^m \otimes \RR^n \cong M_{m \times n}(\RR)$.

The tensor $e_i \otimes f_j$ corresponds to the matrix $E_{ij}$ with 
a 1 in position $(i, j)$ and 0 elsewhere.

content.title: Tensor Products and Linear Maps
content.content:
$V^* \otimes W \cong \mathcal{L}(V, W)$.

The tensor $f \otimes w$ corresponds to the rank-1 linear map 
$v \mapsto f(v) w$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Tensor products provide the algebraic foundation for:

- Multilinear algebra and differential forms

- Tensor fields in differential geometry

- Quantum mechanics (state spaces of composite systems)

- Machine learning (tensor decomposition, neural networks)
